docs.title: DevOps Documentation Hub
docs.text: Welcome to DevOpsVaultX Engineering Wiki, the ultimate central repository built for DevOps users to go from Zero to Hero. Whether you are starting from the absolute basics or architecting enterprise production environments, you will find every detail, command checklist, and advanced blueprint required here.

docs.title: 🌐 Integrated Ecosystem
docs.text: About the Platform: This documentation hub is an extension of DevOpsVaultX Main Site. Our main platform is an open-source, beautifully designed hub of tools and utilities built specifically for developers and DevOps engineers. Because all tools run directly in your browser, your data remains secure, and the execution is completely serverless and instant.

docs.title: 🛠️ Knowledge Tracks
docs.text:
Navigate through our structured documentation categories to speed up your daily engineering workflows:

Track Focus Areas Quick Access 💻 OS Fundamentals Linux & Windows internals, system architectures, and system administration scripts. Explore OS 🔀 Version Control Source code management architectures, structured Git rules, and GitHub workflows. Explore Git 🐳 Containerization Dockerfile runtime optimization, layered builds isolation, and multi-container configs. Explore Docker ☁️ Cloud Platforms AWS and Azure network topographies, multi-tenant compute, and global policies setup. Explore Cloud 🏗️ IaC & Configuration Declarative infrastructure with Terraform states and automated systems setup via Ansible. Explore IaC 🚀 CI/CD Automation Deployment pipelines execution blocks, GitHub Actions runners, and Jenkins files. Explore CI/CD ☸️ Orchestration Kubernetes cluster provisioning configs, ingress tracking controls, and pods management. Explore K8s 📈 Monitoring & Logging Prometheus telemetry pipelines, promQL scrapers, and operational Grafana panels. Explore SRE

docs.title: ⚡ Core Operational Principles
docs.text:
Every guide and utility under the DevOpsVaultX umbrella is built keeping these standard production goals in mind:

  • Security First: Browser-based execution for tools and absolute isolation for pipeline templates.
  • Infrastructure as Code (IaC): Modular, version-controlled architectures that eliminate configuration drift.
  • Performance Engine: Fast, lightweight, and streamlined setups optimized for high-velocity engineering teams.

docs.title: 🤝 Community & Contributions
docs.text:
Found a typo or want to add a production-tested DevOps blueprint?

  1. Fork the documentation repository.
  2. Add your Markdown files to the relevant tracking sections.
  3. Raise a Pull Request (PR) for review.

docs.title: Docker
docs.text: Welcome to the Docker documentation for this project. Use the links below to navigate through the setup, configuration, and architecture details.

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
• Installation Guide – Step-by-step instructions to set up Docker on your machine.
  • Architecture Overview – Details about containers, networks, and volumes used.
  • Command Cheat Sheet – Quick reference for common Docker operations.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
1. Ensure Docker Desktop is installed and running.
  2. Clone the repository.
  3. Open your terminal and run:
   docker compose up -d

docs.title: Docker Architecture
docs.text: This document describes the high-level Docker architecture and container structure used in this project.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Briefly describe how the containers interact with each other and the host system.

docs.title: Container Diagram
docs.text: (Optional: Insert a diagram or ASCII art here)

docs.title: Service Breakdown
docs.text: Service Name Base Image Ports Purpose web-app node:18-alpine 3000:3000 Frontend/Backend application database postgres:15 5432:5432 Persistent data storage

docs.title: Volumes & Persistency
docs.text: • db_data: Named volume mapping to /var/lib/postgresql/data to ensure database persistence.

docs.title: Networks
docs.text: • app_network: Custom bridge network facilitating isolated communication between services.

docs.title: Linux Commands
docs.text: Linux relies heavily on command-line tools. These commands help you manage files, directories, and system settings quickly.

docs.title: Essential Linux Commands
docs.text: Command Description Example ls Lists files and folders in the current directory ls -l cd Changes the current directory cd Documents pwd Prints the path of the current working directory pwd mkdir Creates a new directory (folder) mkdir projects rm Deletes files or directories rm file.txt cp Copies files or directories cp file.txt backup/ mv Moves or renames files or directories mv old.txt new.txt sudo Runs a command with superuser (administrator) privileges sudo apt update

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Use the Tab key to auto-complete your commands or file names.
  • Type clear to clean up your terminal screen.

docs.title: Docker Installation Guide
docs.text: This guide provides step-by-step instructions to install Docker and Docker Compose on Linux, Windows, and macOS.

docs.title: Linux Installation (Ubuntu/Debian)
docs.text: Follow these steps to install Docker Engine using the official repository.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Update your existing list of packages:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y apt-transport-https ca-certificates curl software-properties-common

docs.title: Step 1: Add Docker’s Official GPG Key
docs.text: curl -fsSL [https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg](https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg) | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/docker-archive-keyring.gpg

docs.title: Step 2: Set Up the Repository
docs.text: echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/docker-archive-keyring.gpg] [https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu](https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu) $(lsb_release -cs) stable" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list > /dev/null

docs.title: Step 3: Install Docker Engine
docs.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io docker-compose-plugin

docs.title: Step 4: Manage Docker as a Non-Root User
docs.text:
To run Docker commands without typing sudo:

sudo usermod -aG docker $USER

Note: Log out and log back in for this change to take effect.

docs.title: Windows Installation
docs.text: Docker Desktop on Windows uses WSL 2 (Windows Subsystem for Linux) for better performance.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Windows 10 (64-bit: Pro, Enterprise, or Education) or Windows 11.
  • WSL 2 feature enabled on Windows.

docs.title: Step 1: Download Docker Desktop
docs.text:
Download the official installer from the Docker website:

  • Download Docker Desktop for Windows

docs.title: Step 2: Run the Installer
docs.text:
1. Double-click Docker Desktop Installer.exe to run the wizard.
  2. Ensure the "Use WSL 2 instead of Hyper-V" option is checked on the configuration page.
  3. Follow the instructions, click Close and restart your computer if prompted.

docs.title: Step 3: Start Docker Desktop
docs.text:
• Launch Docker Desktop from the Start menu.
  • Accept the service agreement, and wait until the Docker status icon turns green in your system tray.

docs.title: macOS Installation
docs.text: Docker Desktop is available for both Intel and Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3) Mac chips.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Determine your Mac chip type (Go to Apple Menu -> About This Mac).

docs.title: Step 1: Download Docker Desktop
docs.text:
Select the correct installer based on your processor:

  • Download for Mac with Apple Silicon (M-Series)
  • Download for Mac with Intel Chip

docs.title: Step 2: Installation
docs.text:
1. Double-click the downloaded .dmg file to open it.
  2. Drag the Docker icon into your Applications folder.

docs.title: Step 3: Launch Docker
docs.text:
1. Open Docker from your Applications folder.
  2. Give Docker privileged permissions when prompted (required for networking tools).
  3. The Docker icon will appear in the top menu bar once it is running successfully.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text: To verify that Docker and Docker Compose are installed correctly on any operating system, open your terminal (or PowerShell/Command Prompt) and run:

docs.title: Check Docker Version
docs.text: docker --version

docs.title: Check Docker Compose Version
docs.text: docker compose version

docs.title: Run a Test Container
docs.text: docker run hello-world

docs.title: Note: If you see a "Hello from Docker!" success message, your installation is complete and working correctly.
Linux
docs.text: This roadmap is designed to take you from a Linux beginner to a professional who can confidently manage servers, automate tasks, and deploy applications in a DevOps environment.

docs.title: Phase 1: The Absolute Basics (Foundations)
docs.text: Before jumping into automation, you need to feel comfortable moving around the command line.

docs.title: 1. Essential Navigation & File Management
docs.text:
• Concepts: Understanding the Linux Directory Structure (Everything is a file, /etc, /var, /opt, /home).
  • Commands to Master:
  • pwd (Print working directory)
  • ls -la (List files with details and hidden files)
  • cd (Change directory)
  • mkdir -p (Create nested directories)
  • touch, cp, mv, rm -rf (Create, copy, move, and forcefully delete files/folders)

docs.title: 2. Viewing and Editing Files
docs.text:
• Concepts: Quick viewing vs. interactive editing.
  • Commands to Master:
  • cat, less, head, tail -f (Crucial for monitoring live log files)
  • Vim or Nano: Learn at least one terminal-based text editor. (Know how to open, edit, save, and quit in Vim).

docs.title: Phase 2: User Management & Permissions (Security)
docs.text: In DevOps, you must ensure applications and users have only the minimum required access (Principle of Least Privilege).

docs.title: 1. File Permissions
docs.text:
• Concepts: Read ($r=4$), Write ($w=2$), Execute ($x=1$) for User, Group, and Others.
  • Commands to Master:
  • chmod (Change file permissions, e.g., chmod 755 script.sh)
  • chown (Change file owner and group)

docs.title: 2. User & Sudo Administration
docs.text:
• Concepts: Root user vs. standard user, giving temporary admin rights.
  • Commands to Master:
  • useradd, usermod, userdel
  • passwd (Change password)
  • sudo (Execute command as root)
  • Understanding the /etc/passwd and /etc/sudoers files.

docs.title: Phase 3: Text Processing & Package Management
docs.text: DevOps engineers deal with massive configuration files and logs. You need to know how to filter data quickly.

docs.title: 1. Searching & Filtering
docs.text:
• Commands to Master:
  • grep / egrep (Search for text patterns inside files)
  • find (Locate files based on name, size, or time)
  • awk & sed (Basic stream editing and data extraction)
  • Pipes (|) and Redirections (>, >>): Passing the output of one command as input to another.

docs.title: 2. Package Management
docs.text:
• Concepts: Installing, updating, and removing software dependencies.
  • Tools to Master:
  • Ubuntu/Debian: apt update, apt install, apt remove
  • CentOS/RHEL: yum or dnf equivalents

docs.title: Phase 4: Networking & Troubleshooting
docs.text: Applications talk to each other over networks. When a deployment fails, you need to find out why.

docs.title: 1. Network Inspection
docs.text:
• Concepts: IP addresses, ports, DNS, and firewalls.
  • Commands to Master:
  • ping (Check connectivity)
  • curl -v / wget (Test API endpoints and download files)
  • netstat -tulnp or ss -tulnp (Check which applications are listening on which ports)
  • nslookup / dig (Troubleshoot DNS issues)
  • ip a or ifconfig (Check system network interfaces)

docs.title: Phase 5: Process Management & System Performance
docs.text: How to monitor applications running on your server and deal with resource overloads.

docs.title: 1. Managing Running Services
docs.text:
• Concepts: Systemd init system.
  • Commands to Master:
  • systemctl start | stop | restart | status <service>
  • systemctl enable | disable <service> (Ensure service starts on boot)

docs.title: 2. Resource Monitoring
docs.text:
• Commands to Master:
  • top / htop (Live CPU and memory usage)
  • ps aux | grep <process_name> (Find process IDs)
  • kill -9 <PID> (Force stop a hanging process)
  • df -h (Check available disk space)
  • free -m (Check RAM usage)

docs.title: Phase 6: Automation with Shell Scripting (The DevOps Core)
docs.text: Stop doing things manually. If you do it more than twice, automate it.

docs.title: 1. Scripting Fundamentals
docs.text:
• Topics to Learn:
  • Writing your first #!/bin/bash script.
  • Variables and Environment Variables (export ENV="production").
  • Positional arguments ($1, $2).
  • Conditional statements (if/else) and Loops (for, while).
  • Exit codes (exit 0 vs exit 1).

docs.title: 2. Cron Jobs (Scheduling)
docs.text:
• Concepts: Automating backups, log rotations, or cleanups at specific times.
  • Commands to Master:
  • crontab -e (Edit cron jobs)
  • Understanding the crontab syntax: * * * * * /path/to/script.sh

docs.title: Phase 7: Advanced Linux for DevOps (Bridge to Cloud/Containers)
docs.text: Once you know the OS, learn how it powers modern infrastructure.

docs.title: 1. SSH (Secure Shell)
docs.text:
• Concepts: Passwordless login, managing remote servers safely.
  • Skills to Master:
  • Generating keys: ssh-keygen
  • Copying keys to server: ssh-copy-id user@remote_ip
  • Configuring ~/.ssh/config for easy access.

docs.title: 2. Linux Namespaces & Cgroups (Docker Foundations)
docs.text:
• Concepts: Docker isn't magic; it uses native Linux features.
  • Namespaces: Isolate process views (Network, Mount, PID).
  • Cgroups (Control Groups): Limit resource usage (CPU, Memory).

docs.title: Recommended Practice Project
docs.text:
To lock in your knowledge, set up a local Linux VM (using VirtualBox or AWS EC2 free tier) and do this:

  1. Deploy an Nginx Web Server.
  2. Change its default configuration port to 8080.
  3. Write a Bash script that checks if Nginx is running; if it is down, the script should automatically restart it.
  4. Schedule this script to run every 5 minutes using a Cron Job.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Linux architecture is the structural layout of a Linux operating system. It defines how different parts of the system talk to each other and work together.

docs.title: Components
docs.text:
The Linux system is divided into four major layers:

  1. Hardware: This is the physical part of the computer. It includes your CPU, RAM, hard disk, and other connected devices.

  2. Kernel: The core of the operating system. It directly talks to the hardware. It manages memory, CPU tasks, and disk storage.

  3. Shell: An interface between the user and the kernel. It takes commands from the user, interprets them, and sends them to the kernel to execute.

  4. Applications (User Space): The programs or software that you run on Linux. Examples include web browsers, text editors, and media players.

docs.title: Linux Commands
docs.text: Linux relies heavily on command-line tools. These commands help you manage files, directories, and system settings quickly.

docs.title: Essential Linux Commands
docs.text: Command Description Example ls Lists files and folders in the current directory ls -l cd Changes the current directory cd Documents pwd Prints the path of the current working directory pwd mkdir Creates a new directory (folder) mkdir projects rm Deletes files or directories rm file.txt cp Copies files or directories cp file.txt backup/ mv Moves or renames files or directories mv old.txt new.txt sudo Runs a command with superuser (administrator) privileges sudo apt update

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Use the Tab key to auto-complete your commands or file names.
  • Type clear to clean up your terminal screen.

docs.title: Linux Installation
docs.text: Installing Linux is simple. There are multiple ways to install it depending on your needs.

docs.title: Ways to Install Linux
docs.text:
• Dual Boot: Installing Linux alongside your existing operating system (like Windows). You can choose which OS to open when you start your computer.
  • Virtual Machine (VM): Running Linux inside a software (like VirtualBox or VMware) without removing your current operating system.
  • Live USB: Running Linux directly from a USB drive to try it out without making permanent changes to your computer.

docs.title: Basic Steps for Installation
docs.text:
1. Download an ISO file: Choose a Linux distribution (like Ubuntu, Fedora, or Mint) and download its ISO file from the official website.
  2. Create a Bootable USB: Use tools like Rufus or BalenaEtcher to flash the ISO file onto a USB drive.
  3. Boot from USB: Restart your computer, enter the BIOS menu, and select your USB drive to boot.
  4. Follow the On-Screen Instructions: Click "Install", choose your language, format your disk partition, create a username and password, and complete the installation.
